title: How to Build Unshakable Confidence and Rise Up Without Permission
guestBio: Nada Lena Nasserdeen is the Founder & CEO of Rise Up For You, author, executive coach, and former college professor. She is a global advocate for leadership, confidence, and self-worth who has helped thousands of professionals rise into their full potential from the classroom to the boardroom.
excerpt: Confidence isn't a personality trait—it's a skill you can develop. Join Aimee and executive coach Nada Nasserdeen as they explore how to overcome imposter syndrome, build unshakable confidence, and step into leadership without waiting for permission.
metaDescription: Learn how to build confidence, overcome imposter syndrome, and rise into leadership with insights from executive coach Nada Nasserdeen on The Manage Her.
keyTakeaways: Confidence is a learnable skill, not an innate personality trait that you either have or don't have.
Modern leadership requires authenticity and the courage to bridge the gap between who you are and who you're meant to be.
Overcoming imposter syndrome starts with shifting your mindset and recognizing your inherent worth and capabilities.
Rising up in leadership means stopping playing small and stepping into your purpose without waiting for permission.
Building unshakable confidence requires consistent practice and the willingness to embrace discomfort as part of growth.
Visibility and leadership challenges are shared experiences among women that can be overcome with the right strategies.
Your diverse background and experiences are assets that shape your unique vision and leadership approach.

pullQuotes.text: Confidence is a skill, not a personality trait — and leadership begins when we stop playing small and start rising into our purpose.

timestamps.label: Nada's Origin Story and Rise Up For You
Bridging the Gap Between Who You Are and Who You're Meant to Be

content:
What if I told you that the confidence you've been waiting to magically appear is actually a skill you can develop right now? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with the incredible Nada Lena Nasserdeen, Founder & CEO of Rise Up For You, to explore exactly how you can build unshakable confidence and step into leadership without waiting for anyone's permission.

Nada's journey from college professor to executive coach to global advocate for women's leadership is a masterclass in reinvention and purpose-driven pivoting. Her diverse background has shaped her unique vision for helping thousands of professionals bridge the gap between who they are and who they're meant to be. This isn't just feel-good advice—this is strategic, actionable guidance for women ready to rise up.

The Truth About Confidence That Changes Everything

Here's what you need to hear: confidence isn't something you're born with or without. It's not a personality trait that some lucky people get while others are left wondering why they feel so uncertain. Confidence is a skill, and like any skill, it can be developed, strengthened, and mastered through intentional practice.

This reframe is revolutionary because it puts the power back in your hands. You're not waiting for confidence to strike like lightning—you're actively building it through your choices, your actions, and your willingness to step outside your comfort zone. Every time you speak up in a meeting, every time you share your ideas, every time you take on a challenge that scares you a little, you're building your confidence muscle.

Rising Up Without Permission

One of the most powerful themes Nada explores is the concept of rising up without permission. Too often, women wait for someone else to validate their readiness for leadership, to give them the green light to pursue their dreams, or to confirm that they're qualified enough to take up space. But here's the truth: no one is coming to give you permission because you already have it.

This mindset shift requires you to stop playing small and start recognizing that your voice, your perspective, and your leadership are needed right now. You don't need another certification, another year of experience, or another person's approval. You need to trust yourself and take action from where you are with what you have.

The conversation between Aimee and Nada reveals how this permission-free approach to leadership isn't just about individual success—it's about creating ripple effects that benefit entire organizations and communities. When you step into your power, you give others permission to do the same.

Conquering Imposter Syndrome Once and for All

Imposter syndrome—that nagging voice that tells you you're not qualified, that you're going to be found out, that you're somehow fooling everyone around you—is one of the biggest barriers to leadership that women face. But Nada offers a different perspective on how to handle these feelings when they arise.

Instead of trying to eliminate imposter syndrome completely, she suggests recognizing it as a sign that you're growing and challenging yourself. The goal isn't to never feel like an imposter again; it's to feel it and move forward anyway. This approach transforms imposter syndrome from a stop sign into a compass pointing toward your next area of growth.

The key is understanding that feeling unprepared or uncertain doesn't mean you actually are unprepared or unqualified. It often means you're stepping into something bigger than what you've done before, and that's exactly where growth happens. Your discomfort is data, not a reason to retreat.

Modern Leadership Requires Authentic Connection

The conversation also dives deep into what modern leadership actually looks like in today's world. Gone are the days when leadership meant having all the answers, maintaining perfect composure, or leading through intimidation and hierarchy. Today's most effective leaders are those who lead with authenticity, vulnerability, and genuine connection.

This is particularly powerful for women who have been told that emotion has no place in leadership or that they need to choose between being liked and being respected. Modern leadership embraces the full spectrum of human experience and recognizes that connecting with people on a genuine level is actually a strategic advantage, not a weakness.

Nada emphasizes that the traits many women have been conditioned to see as limitations—empathy, collaboration, intuition—are actually superpowers in today's leadership landscape. The world needs leaders who can navigate complexity with both logic and emotional intelligence, who can build bridges instead of walls, and who can inspire others through authentic connection rather than fear-based authority.

Your Diverse Background is Your Competitive Advantage

One of the most encouraging aspects of Nada's story is how her diverse background—from academia to coaching to entrepreneurship—has become her greatest asset rather than a source of confusion or scattered focus. This is a powerful message for women who worry that their non-linear career paths or varied interests make them less qualified for leadership roles.

Your diverse experiences give you a unique perspective that can't be replicated. They allow you to see connections others miss, to relate to a wider range of people, and to bring innovative solutions to complex problems. Instead of apologizing for your varied background, you can leverage it as evidence of your adaptability, curiosity, and broad skill set.

This conversation is a reminder that there's no single path to leadership and no perfect formula for success. Your journey, with all its twists and turns, has been preparing you for exactly where you are right now and exactly where you're going next.

As you listen to this episode, you'll find yourself nodding along to the shared challenges women face around visibility, self-advocacy, and stepping into leadership roles. But more importantly, you'll walk away with concrete strategies for building the unshakable confidence you need to rise up and lead without apology. The world is waiting for what you have to offer—and it's time to stop waiting for permission to share it.

quiz.title: What's Your Confidence Building Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach building confidence and stepping into your power. Find out which strategies from episode 14 will help you rise up without permission.

questions.question: When you're facing a new challenge or opportunity that feels intimidating, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Research everything I can about it and create a detailed plan of action
Trust my gut feeling and jump in, figuring it out as I go
Talk it through with trusted friends or mentors to get their perspectives
Start with a small, manageable version of the challenge to test the waters

questions.question: How do you typically handle moments when you're doubting yourself or feeling like an imposter?

options.text: I remind myself of my qualifications and all the work I've put in to be here
I focus on why I'm passionate about this and trust that my authentic self is enough
I reach out to my support network and remember I'm not alone in feeling this way
I acknowledge the feeling and take one small confident action to shift my energy

questions.question: What gives you the strongest sense of confidence in professional settings?

options.text: Knowing I'm exceptionally well-prepared and knowledgeable about the topic
Staying true to my authentic voice and unique perspective
Feeling connected to the people around me and knowing we're in it together
Building on past successes and proven track record

questions.question: When you think about 'rising up without permission,' what resonates most with you?

options.text: Having all my ducks in a row so no one can question my readiness
Following my inner knowing even when others don't understand my vision
Creating space for other women to rise up alongside me
Taking progressively bigger steps until I'm exactly where I want to be

questions.question: How do you prefer to step outside your comfort zone?

options.text: With a clear strategy and backup plan in place
By following what feels aligned and exciting, even if it's uncertain
With encouragement and support from people who believe in me
By gradually expanding what feels comfortable over time

questions.question: What would make you feel most ready to take on a bigger leadership role?

options.text: Additional training, certifications, or expertise in key areas
A clear sense of purpose and mission that I'm passionate about
A strong team and network of supporters who can help me succeed
More experience and proven wins in progressively responsible roles

results.title: The Strategic Preparer
results.description: You build confidence through thorough preparation and mastering your craft. You believe in doing your homework and showing up ready, which is a tremendous strength. Your methodical approach means when you do step forward, you're incredibly well-equipped. The key for you is learning when 'good enough' is actually perfect, and that sometimes the best preparation is simply taking action. This episode will show you how to bridge the gap between your incredible preparation and the bold action that's waiting for you.

results.title: The Intuitive Leap-Taker
results.description: You're naturally drawn to following your instincts and taking inspired action, even when you can't see the full picture. Your willingness to trust yourself is a rare and powerful gift in leadership. You understand that confidence comes from doing, not just thinking. Your challenge might be honoring your intuitive wisdom when others question your unconventional approach. This episode will validate your natural confidence-building style and give you language to articulate why your approach works so brilliantly.

results.title: The Community Builder
results.description: You gain confidence through connection, collaboration, and lifting others as you rise. You understand that vulnerability creates strength and that authentic relationships fuel sustainable success. Your ability to see confidence as a collective experience rather than a solo journey is exactly what modern leadership needs. Sometimes you might doubt whether your collaborative approach is 'leadership enough,' but it absolutely is. This episode will reinforce why your relationship-centered confidence style is not just valid, but essential for the future of leadership.

results.title: The Gradual Expander
results.description: You build confidence by taking consistent small steps and celebrating incremental progress. You understand that sustainable growth happens through steady expansion of your comfort zone, not dramatic leaps. Your approach creates lasting confidence because it's built on a solid foundation of proven experiences. You might sometimes feel pressure to move faster or be bolder, but your methodical approach is actually incredibly wise. This episode will help you see how your steady confidence-building style is a strength, while also showing you how to recognize when you're ready for bigger steps than you think.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey, Nada. Today I'm losing my voice. So, I'm going to have you introduce yourself. Can you tell us a little bit about what you do?

**Nada:** Absolutely. Well, it's an honor to be here, Aimee. I can completely understand losing the voice. You had a big day. You got your book launch. We got the podcast going. Congratulations, by the way.

**Aimee:** Oh, thank you so much.

**Nada:** Of course. So I'm Nada Lena Nasserdeen. I'm the CEO and founder of Rise Up for You. I'm a motivational speaker and a best-selling author. And essentially what I do is my team and I work with leaders, entrepreneurs, and organizations around the world to enhance their performance and help them be the top 10% in what they do through soft skills. 

So a lot of times as individuals, we spend most of our time, money, and energy building technical skills, going to school, getting our degrees, but we don't spend a lot of time building emotional intelligence, self-confidence, how to be transformational leadership, how to communicate in an influential way. And my team and I are doing that on a day-to-day basis.

**Aimee:** That's amazing and so important nowadays when we're losing touch with one another in a generation that has been raised on screens and social media and those kinds of things. Super critical.

**Nada:** With everything that's happened in the last 5 years and technology and artificial intelligence and screen time, emotional intelligence and soft skills are going to be the things that help you stay competitive. It's also going to be the body of skills that are going to help you build a life where you have it all. Because we all know a lot of people that are super successful in maybe one area of their life and they've doubled down on that one area, but if you really - I always say happy, healthy, wealthy, sexy, and making an impact - if you got these skills, you're amazing.

**Aimee:** Thank you so much for coming in today. And I know you came all the way - you're not even from California anymore.

**Nada:** Not anymore. I was. I used to live here. I spent a lot of time here. I was here from 5 to what, 35, 36. So, a little over 30 years. And then I recently moved to Las Vegas, Nevada, which I love and it's been awesome there. But I would say probably about 50% of my team are still in California.

**Aimee:** Amazing. So you just happen to be in San Diego today doing your thing?

**Nada:** Yeah, we have a couple things actually. We have a lot going on this week here in California. We're doing some work in the county up north. And then I did a keynote today in San Diego for leaders for one of the counties specifically on emotional and social intelligence. And on our way back, we're here.

**Aimee:** I love it. Thank you so much for taking time out of your busy schedule. It means so much to me.

**Nada:** We're all busy. So yeah.

**Aimee:** Well, I remember when I was writing my book, I met you at a We Bank West event and here you were Nada, she is a best-selling author. And I'm like, you gave me your number and you were like, just call me. And I called you and you were like, you can do this. And thank you so much because I needed that. I needed that word. To do something new, to hop out of your protective bubble that you live in, it's really scary.

**Nada:** It is. Well, you should be very proud of yourself because one of the things that I'm really passionate about that I talk about a lot is self-confidence. And most individuals - and I would say about 83% because we ask every year - 83% of individuals struggle with self-confidence and it impacts their ability to take action. 

And so there's many individuals like you Aimee, women, men, it doesn't matter that they want to - they've been wanting to write the book for years or they've been wanting to build the business and they still haven't because of the thoughts or the fear or whatever belief system is telling them that they can't. And so you should be really proud of yourself that you actually wrote the book.

**Aimee:** And writing it was actually the easy part. The harder part was actually taking it out into the world. That was so scary to make the leap to actually push the button that says, "Okay, we're here, we are, we're live and anyone can buy your book and it's out there." That was for me and I wasn't anticipating that at all. I really thought writing the book would be the hardest part.

**Nada:** Good for you. Yeah. Congratulations.

**Aimee:** Yeah, thank you. Anytime I come against fear, I'm always like, "Okay, you're about to do something you've never done before. Let's go." And it's not life-threatening fear like when we were in the caveman era and our amygdala in our brain was like - it's a good fear. And it's kind of invigorating. And in the world that we live in, sometimes I feel like we're not challenged enough. We are so comfortable, we're so entertained, we're so pacified.

**Nada:** Yes, that's true. And when you get those feelings of being excited, butterflies, the nerves, you get kind of hooked on it. Well, unfortunately, most people aren't living. They're on autopilot. So these feelings that you're talking about of invigoration and passion and life, unfortunately many individuals are walking around in zombie mode.

And I think that we all - I know for me and I could see the passion in you - we thrive to have this energy of this urgency. There's this feeling in me that's like a constant urgent feeling of we got to go. We have so much to do in this world to make an impact, to live, to feel every aspect.

I once did a keynote and usually when I keynote there's no other keynotes, right? So I go on, I do my thing and then that's it, then they have breakouts. But I once did a keynote and there was another keynote there. So I had the pleasure of watching. I was like oh this is great. I get to watch another speaker. And she said something that has stuck with me ever since and she said every single day I want to commit to dying empty. So if I don't wake up tomorrow then I know that today I gave everything. I left it all here. I have no regrets and all my passion, all my energy I gave it all in the day.

**Aimee:** I love that. I was very inspired when at the very end of writing my book the very last chapters came through - it was coming from that place - this is my legacy. Why would I not want to leave this behind for my kids, for other people, for the people that will come after me? This is - I get to leave a little something.

**Nada:** Exactly. And finding that passion just in that for the next generation, for that one person that might need it. And there are a lot of people that need it. It's like if you can just be brave just for that one person and then you end up being brave for yourself.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Well, and then it gives permission for other people to be brave. Because there's so many people that are just kind of waiting and then they see you do it and they're like, "Okay, I can do it."

**Nada:** Yeah. It's like as we rise, we can each pull up maybe one or two more people along the way, right?

**Aimee:** 100%. And then they pull up one or two and they pull and over time it's a positive trickle effect.

**Nada:** I love it. I just think that it's - and you pulled me up. Thank you. You really did. It was your inspiration. It really was. Thank you so much for telling me, for giving me the permission, for showing me that it was doable. It was meant to be. We met for a reason. 

And I remember right away you're like, "You wrote a book?" I'm like, "Yes," because here I was at that point in my life, I had this book that was pouring out of me non-stop. And I was like, "Okay, great. I have this book. What do I do with it?" I wrote this stuff in my notes in my phone - what's next?

**Aimee:** Yeah. Exactly. Congratulations. Thank you so much. So, could you share with me what inspired you to found Rise Up for You and how your background influenced this decision?

**Nada:** Yeah, it was a lot actually. So, I used to be a performer actually. I used to do musical theater and I used to tour internationally around the world and sing and dance. That's where it started. I remember when I was a performer, I always got the center stage spot in the room, but I was never the best singer. So, I started to understand at a younger age that it was more than my technical skill. I practiced, I worked hard, I was a great singer, but I was never the best.

And so a lot of times I would get the main part of the solo or the center stage, but I was always confused because all these other men and women could sing me out of the water. And so I started to kind of observe why am I the one that's getting the solo or the center stage? And I realized very quickly that it wasn't my voice that was landing me that spot. It was my confidence or going to the director and being like, "Hey, can you give me feedback? I want to be better." It was these other aspects that we now called soft skills that I didn't know they were called that at the time that I was tapping into.

Now, fast forward about 7, 8 years, I become the executive of an education corporation and I'm leading about 200 people under my team and I did that for a while and I loved it. And I saw the same challenge happening. So, professionals that had PhDs, master's degrees, bachelors, you name it, all this stuff were really struggling, not only professionally but personally. They just were not happy. They were unfulfilled. They were not engaged. It impacted their leadership. They struggled with perfectionist mindset and imposter syndrome and feeling enough. And all of these things infiltrated the workplace. But you could also tell that when they went home, they were unhappy, and that was the majority of the workforce.

And so I started to just question why is this happening? And I started to pursue the knowledge of leadership and how to push your potential and your fulfillment. And then everything came crashing down. So I decided to resign from the company after about 5, 6 years. And I sold my house, sold my car, I was an executive, got rid of everything. And I moved out of the country for an opportunity called marriage.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Nada:** Yeah. I know. And within 4 weeks, my husband said he wanted a divorce on the first day of our honeymoon.

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh.

**Nada:** So, I lost everything in the matter of a couple weeks. I lost all my money. All I had was a hundred bucks. Didn't have a house, didn't have a car, no job because I replaced myself. And I came back to California and I had two luggage.

**Aimee:** Oh my god.

**Nada:** And I remember being on the plane and I fell asleep because I was crying so much. I was in shock. And my father came to me in my dream because my father died when I was 27. And he came to me in my dream and he said, "Nada, everything you need is already inside of you. You just have to rise up for you."

**Aimee:** No way.

**Nada:** Yeah. And so that phrase just kept sticking out in my mind. I'm like, rise up, rise up for you, rise up for you. So I came back to California, didn't know anything about entrepreneurship, knew nothing about business, but this idea of rise up for you kept coming into my head. So I was like, well, I've got nothing to lose because I lost it all.

**Aimee:** It's kind of the best place to be sometimes.

**Nada:** So, I decided that I was going to start to build Rise Up for You, but I didn't know what it was going to be yet. I just really just had the name, but I knew I wanted it to be empowering and educational because that's where I come from. And so, I started building Rise Up for You. I started with the podcast. It's all I had was just free knowledge and education.

And 3 months later, my second parent, my mother was diagnosed with stage four cancer. So, I built the company out of a hospital. I took showers at the 24-hour fitness in Newport Beach, Balboa area, and I would go back to the hospital, Hoag Hospital, open up my laptop. I had no clue what I was doing. Who's Tony Robbins? I didn't know any of that stuff.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Nada:** 9 months later, she died. So, by the time I was 31 years old, I buried both my parents, going through a divorce, lost all my money. I was at rock bottom. But I had Rise Up for You. I had the name. I had the podcast, right? That's really all I had.

And I just remember asking myself what does it mean? What does it mean to build a life that you're proud of? Both of my parents worked really, really hard. They both died when they were 63. They didn't even make it to retirement. They were beautiful individuals. They weren't heavy. Sickness somehow got them. And so, I just started to question what is this really all about?

And from there, I just doubled down on my self-confidence. That's the one thing that I've always had. And I had the name Rise Up for You. And I just started building and I never looked back. And today, we're in over 50 countries. I have an amazing team. They do most of the training and the coaching today. And today I primarily do the train the trainer program, building the next pipeline of our trainers and coaches and the keynoting.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. Look at you go. So proud. From the ashes you rise.

**Nada:** That's right.

**Aimee:** Like the phoenix. Good for you. With your experience as a college professor and an executive, how did you transition into entrepreneurship and coaching?

**Nada:** It was really hard. But the coaching is not hard and the training is not hard because it's innate in me. What was difficult was just understanding business, just the landscape of business. I'm always proud of the fact that I built it with a hundred bucks. I didn't have a business degree. I didn't have investors or none of that. I didn't have any capital, no seed funding. I literally had $100 and was like, "All right, let's figure this out."

What I did first with Rise Up for You is I said, "Okay, what do I know and what am I good at?" Well, I'm really damn good on the mic because I used to be a singer. So, for me, I'm like, "Well, I'm not afraid to talk."

**Aimee:** That's amazing.

**Nada:** I'm not afraid to talk. I'm not afraid of a microphone because I used to perform. So, you never know what your skills are going to - how they're going to help you in the future, right?

**Aimee:** It's so true.

**Nada:** And then the other thing that I knew I was good at because I asked people was pushing people's potential and teaching. So, when I asked people, I'm like, "What am I good at?" Every single person said, "You're really good at seeing potential in other people and you're a great teacher." When you're up front teaching - when I used to teach the other performers for example and I used to cast shows and teach about the voice and the pedagogy and the dance moves - I was always rated as one of the top teachers.

So I was like okay well I'm really good on the mic. I'm good speaking. I see that I'm a good trainer and good with teaching. And the problem that I saw is soft skills. It's people not knowing how to push their potential and therefore they can't pass that potential on to anybody else. You can't lead, you can't give when you don't have it yourself.

And so that's kind of where I started. And it was hard. I didn't know anything about business, but I believed in myself. And when you believe in yourself, you can do a lot because I just - I remember driving in the car, no money, and I heard this podcast and there was this guy and he was doing this event in Vegas, no money. And I'm like I drove to Vegas and I just went. It was this entrepreneurial podcast that I heard and they're like we're doing a business conference. Come learn how to build your business and make an impact. And I'm like okay I don't - I'm going to drive. I have no money but I'm going.

And I just started to learn and I would stay up multiple hours 2, 3:00 a.m. How do you build a website? And I just started building it and really just kind of came from there.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. You just wanted to build it so you built it.

**Nada:** Yeah. And I've never doubted myself. There were a lot of times where I was like, I don't know what I'm doing. But I never doubted that I could learn it. I was like, okay, I just got to learn it.

**Aimee:** And I feel like nowadays there's even more tools to learn - using ChatGPT when you don't know something. There's so many tools that we can lean into with technology now when we don't know how to do something.

**Nada:** 100%. And I think I always knew how to prepare. The first client we ever had as a company, Rise Up for You was a Fortune 3 company.

**Aimee:** Wow. So, I'll let you decide which one you think it is.

**Nada:** Wow. And they didn't know that. But somebody saw the podcast and the posts and stuff I was doing on social media. I wasn't making any money. I was still just building content. And I got an email that was like, "Hey, we heard you were an amazing coach and you do this great work and we'd love to hire you for this thing and it was a lot of money." And I thought it was spam. I was like, "Oh, okay." And then they were like, "No, no, we want to interview. We want to meet you."

And I remember I was like, "Oh my god, I've never had a client before. I've never made any money. And now I'm going to go into this interview with these people that are at this massive company to see if they're going to bring me in." I wrote down every freaking possible question they could ask me. What could they ask me in this meeting? And I studied it and then I went through my experiences and I'm like, "Oh yeah, I did this in corporate." And I just prepped and then I went and I did the interview and 30 minutes later they were like, "We got the job and we want you to bring a team member."

Now I don't - I'm a team of one. I'm like not a problem. Got it. What kind of team? How many do you want?

**Aimee:** Yes, sir.

**Nada:** And the rest is history and 5 years later we're still working with them every year.

**Aimee:** Oh my god, that's amazing. Because sometimes it is fake it till you make it. You knew you had something to give. You knew you had something that was important to give and you just gave it.

**Nada:** Well, and it's also knowing how to position yourself. I might not have spoke for thousands of people in corporate, let's say, at that time, but I've spoken and stood on stage for hundreds of thousands of people as a singer. So, it's like, yeah, I've been on stage for hundreds of thousands of people. Now, whether it's me singing and answering a question about voice versus me standing on stage and talking about being your best, to me, it's same same.

**Aimee:** Same same. Amazing. You're amazing. I love it. So, your work has impacted thousands of people globally. What do you believe is the key to empowering individuals in their personal and professional lives?

**Nada:** From a coach and training perspective, if we're on this end, I'm a firm believer that - I know we talked about this a little bit earlier - you can't give what you don't have. So, I think what makes somebody a great coach and a great trainer is somebody that has really high self-confidence.

Now, let me just clarify. Self-confidence is not ugly. It's not mean. It's not arrogant. It's not possible to be overly confident because true self-confidence - if you look up the definition - is a faith and a certainty in oneself. It's a self-awareness. It's a humbleness. It's an understanding that when you make mistakes and you fail, that's not your identity and that doesn't define you. True self-confidence, it's not competitive with anybody else. We want everyone to win.

And so when you have high self-confidence, you have the capacity to see other people. When you don't have high self-confidence, everything that you're doing is through a lens of I'm not good enough. What are you trying to say about me? Why did you just trigger me? Are you trying to tell me something? There's always this lens of I'm not good enough and so it impacts how you coach and how you train the person in front of you.

I always say this when it comes to speaking - a lot of people have a fear of public speaking, right? It's one of the number one fears. And a lot of people ask me when you go on stage - I just get in my zone and I go. And I'm like well that's because I'm not thinking about myself, where most people do think about themselves when they're on stage, right? But I'm like when I'm on stage it's not about me. It's about you. And you have to be able to have self-confidence to get to that place.

**Aimee:** To be able to be on stage and be like I'm not thinking if you like me. I'm not thinking if you like my dress. All I'm thinking about is how can I serve you?

**Nada:** Yes. And that's where you make transformation with the clients or whoever is sitting in front of you.

**Aimee:** Wow, that's profound and so true. I see that in my everyday life. That's amazing. So, in your book, you talk about closing the gap between your potential and your performance. Can you elaborate on the concept?

**Nada:** Yeah. So I talk about closing the gap between you and your potential. Because many of us our potential is way up here. But we set our thermometer at 70. Ed Mylett talks a lot about this and I really appreciate how he words it and he essentially says many of us we keep functioning at the 70 mark or the 75 mark at the thermostat and then when we push our potential then we get to 80, we get to 85 and we're getting hotter and we're growing and we're pushing our potential and then we freak out and we drop back down to the 75 mark. We drop back down to the 70 mark, right?

We see this most predominantly when it comes to health. I'm going to get fit. I'm going to lose 50 pounds. You lose 50 pounds and then you self-sabotage because you're not comfortable with success. You're not comfortable with pushing your potential that then you gain the 50 lbs back and another 20 and you go back down to the thermostat, right, of 70.

We see this when it comes to relationships. We've all had friends or family members that they finally meet the one that we're like, "Oh, they are perfect for you. They're kind or loving" and the person freaks out and they just sabotage the relationship even though we can all see that that was the one, right? Because they weren't ready to push themselves and they drop back down to that 70 on the thermostat.

And so my book is really all about that. It's how do you push back past that place of comfort so that you can elevate yourself and your potential and continue to pursue your knowledge and your growth.

**Aimee:** And that's huge. It's so important right now, especially for women. And all people. But I really feel like addressing that for women is really big and that will be really complimentary to the manager community.

**Nada:** Well, a lot of women - obviously we work with a lot of women and we work with a lot of men. Men struggle just as much. They just deal with it differently. But a lot of women when they struggle with self-confidence and imposter syndrome and perfectionist mindset, often times they get crippled in taking action, right?

In men, sometimes we see the opposite. When a man is not confident, sometimes they assert anyways. They move forward anyway, right? We saw this with the Hewlett and Packard statistic. They said that men will apply for a job when they have 60% of the knowledge and women won't apply until they have 100%.

**Aimee:** Interesting.

**Nada:** So, it's like a D minus versus an A+, right? And so, we see this often is that women unfortunately struggle a lot more with perfectionist mindset, with imposter syndrome, with feeling enough. Unfortunately, even in today's world, there's still a scarcity mindset around other women sitting at the table with you. And so, then that creates behaviors in us. And there's a lot of women on women in the workplace. I'll be honest with you. The majority of female executives that I've worked with or the majority of female clients that I've worked with that are struggling that struggle with confidence, it's because of an ugly situation they had with a female leader.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. I think that's a big part of what needs to be addressed is the woman-on-woman toxicity as well. In my life and my career I have been so lifted up by men. Women as well. But there have been significant progress and milestones that were made in my life that were given to me by male mentors. And this movement isn't at all about putting men down or any of that. It's really about how we as women can learn to really cooperate with one another and really help one another and mentor one another and help each other get to the next level.

**Nada:** 100%. I mean I come from a family so all my siblings are men. So I come from a very male-dominated family but all my mom, my aunts, my cousins were all so empowering as females. So I feel lucky that I'm like okay I had the best of both worlds. It's like I understand how to work with men because all my brothers are men. But I had very powerful women that were in my life as well.

But when I went into the workplace really any industry that I was in I never had a challenge with men. They never pushed me down. But I did have challenges with women that either felt threatened or they just were afraid to make space. And I always thought that that was interesting, but now I'm older and obviously when I became an executive, I understood, right? Now you understand the behavior and the psychology and kind of what's going on with people.

**Aimee:** I have had some men do predatorial things as far as in business - in my 20s, especially when I was young and full of ideas, didn't know how to protect them. But as far as the head-to-head combat has been more with the females. Even in film school. Very interesting. But just that feeling of not enough seats at the table.

**Nada:** And it's not malicious. It's just that there really is a fear that I've worked so hard to get here and there's only so many seats. But that doesn't exist as much today. And most of us, there is room at the table for multiple women. And it's better if we pull each other up because I firmly believe that women are stronger together.

**Aimee:** I do too. I believe that's truly our nature. I feel like women have always worked in circles. And I feel like the more that we can bring that tradition back - we lost it for a minute and I'd love to bring that back. And we take care of each other. My team is split 50/50. And shout out to all of the team members and they're all amazing. Not to knock my male team members. You guys are great, too. But the team members that are female, they're so nurturing all the time. How can I help you? What do you need? And I think that's really special, too. It really is. It's a special gift that women have. It's just the way we're born.

**Aimee:** How do you define leadership and what qualities do you believe are essential for effective leaders today?

**Nada:** That's a really good question. For me, leadership is transformative, not transactional. A lot of leaders unfortunately function at a transactional level. And the way that I like to picture it is like Maslow's hierarchy. So the bottom of Maslow's hierarchy is resources, support, safety, do you feel good? A lot of leaders are in that space. It's like, "How you doing today?" "Good. Do you need anything? Everything's okay. I have an open door policy. Let me know." Right? That's where they are.

I think the greatest leaders are transformative. They take you to the top of Maslow's hierarchy, which is self-actualization, which is helping the individual push their potential. The greatest leaders are potential seekers. They look at their team and they say, "There's more in there. I'm going to get you" and there's a fire in them to do that.

So for me that's the greatest - those are the greatest leaders and I'm always going to go back to self-confidence. I think emotional intelligence is the number one factor leaders need to be successful. Not just leaders, anybody needs to be successful. I truly believe it's what helps you become the top 10% in anything that you do and in your life. And self-confidence is one of the major areas that if we can get it down, it'll change their leadership.

But a lot of people are confused with what that means, self-confidence. Here's what it looks like in the workplace. Somebody says something to you and you get triggered and then you behave unprofessionally. Why do you get triggered? Because whatever they said to you set you off or made you feel a certain way. Maybe they didn't make you feel enough or maybe they didn't validate you, right? It's a self-confidence thing.

Have you ever had a team member where you gave them feedback on an email and they just came crashing down, right? They started crying and bawling because they don't feel enough when you talk to them about the email. Instead of them hearing the email, they hear I'm not enough. It becomes about them. So, these are just some of the things - leaders that are - I've had executives of companies that are sitting in meetings and have something to say and they don't say it. I'm like, "You're an executive." They're like, "Just, I didn't know if people would like it."

And so, there's all these little things where it shows up in the workplace that impact the leading and the ability to create the transformation that we need.
